learnings.note: Root-caused the POS-wizard e2e blocker (#68 employee onboarding + #3 booking-create + #12 full-user-journey). The real cause was NOT a stale selector or a bookable-count timing issue: the e2e DB's Department table was EMPTY. The base dev seed (apps/backend/prisma/seed.ts) seeds only admin/branch/configs; the 'عيادات سواء' department comes from a SEPARATE manual script (prisma/seeds/sawa-customer.ts → seed:sawa) the Playwright harness never runs. With zero departments, the e2e seed created the shared 'Test Category' orphaned (departmentId:null), the wizard's department step (step-department.tsx) rendered zero cards, and the helper's department-card click hung until the 120s timeout. Proven empirically: GET /dashboard/organization/departments returned total:0 and the reused Test Category had departmentId:null.
learnings.lesson: An e2e flow that drives the booking POS wizard depends on a clinic DEPARTMENT existing, but no harness-run seed guarantees it. Make the e2e seed self-sufficient: ensure the clinic department exists (find-or-create), attach/re-parent the test category to it, and bust the departments cache after seeding a bookable service. Don't assume `seed:sawa` ran. Verify backend reference-table state (GET departments/categories) with a tiny repro script before guessing at the UI layer.

learnings.note: After the wizard chain was fixed, full-user-journey #12 surfaced THREE more stale assertions in sequence, each newly reachable: (1) POST /dashboard/finance/invoices returned 409 INVOICE_ALREADY_EXISTS because a dashboard-confirmed booking auto-creates its invoice (bookings.booking.confirmed → BookingConfirmedHandler) — product-correct, so the seed's createInvoice() was made idempotent (fetch the existing invoice on 409); (2) the auto-invoice uses DEFAULT_VAT_RATE=0, so total==subtotal (28000→280.00), not the test's hand-computed 32200 — expected totals realigned; (3) the bookings list has no delivery-type column and the payments list renders status via the i18n label 'مدفوع' not raw 'COMPLETED'. All three were test debt against the real product, fixed test-side.
learnings.lesson: When an e2e UI step starts passing for the first time, it exposes the NEXT untested assumption (auto-invoicing, default-VAT totals, i18n status labels, removed columns). Expect a cascade and verify each against real handlers/i18n. A booking confirmed through the dashboard already owns exactly one invoice (@@unique([bookingId])) at 0% VAT — seeds creating invoices for such bookings must be idempotent.

learnings.note: While fixing a stale e2e selector (#68 employee onboarding), I burned 3 full prod-build run cycles (~6 min each) guessing selectors one layer at a time. Each guess uncovered the NEXT stale layer: removed 'custom pricing' switch -> stale durationOptions assertion -> dept name regex -> finally a disabled WizardCard. Reading step-department.tsx up front (the dept renders as <button disabled={bookableCategoriesCount===0}>, so Playwright .click waits for enabled forever) would have revealed the real blocker in one read instead of three runs.
learnings.lesson: To fix a stale e2e selector, READ the target component's current render first (element type, role, disabled gating, conditional mount) before re-running. One component read beats three expensive prod-build runs. And in describe.serial, the first test must fully pass for siblings to run, so a deep multi-layer failure blocks the whole block (the did-not-run sibling).

learnings.note: Dashboard Playwright flows MUST run on a prod build (PW_E2E_PROD=1 -> next build && next start), never the Turbopack dev server. First attempt reused the running `next dev` :5203 server which crashed between the auth-setup phase and the first test -> all 35 smoke tests failed ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED while the 4 setup tests passed. Re-running with PW_E2E_PROD=1 gave a clean signal: smoke 39/39, flows 145/163. The passing setup tests were the tell that auth/seed/API were healthy and only the runtime was dead.
learnings.lesson: If dashboard smoke fails with ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED but the auth-setup tests pass, it is a dead/crashed dev server, not a product bug -> switch to PW_E2E_PROD=1 and re-run once. reuseExistingServer silently reuses a flaky dev server, so verify the server under test is the prod build before declaring any e2e result.

learnings.note: Triaging 13 dashboard e2e flow failures: 12 were test-debt, 1 a genuine product question. Verified each against real app code/seed/i18n: '#__next' is a Pages-Router root absent in this App-Router app; asserting raw enum 'COMPLETED' ignores the i18n label; exact regex /^عيادات$/ misses seed name 'عيادات سواء'; '.last()'/class-based selectors are brittle. Only the session-expiry redirect (auth) was escalated, and it is owner-gated.
learnings.lesson: A failing dashboard e2e flow is guilty-until-proven for the TEST, not the product: check selector vs current DOM, assertion vs i18n label (not raw enum), name regex vs actual seed value, and brittle .last()/CSS targets. Escalate to a product bug only after app code/seed/i18n rule out a stale test; if it touches auth, gate it owner-only.

learnings.note: bugs.json carried BUG-001 (assertCanAssignRole <=) and BUG-002 (RemoveRoleHandler no rank gate) as OPEN P1 security gaps, sourced from the 2026-06-23 lessons.md which recorded them as 'not patched, owner approval required'. When the owner approved fixing them, re-reading the CURRENT source showed BOTH were already remediated in dedicated prior commits (5f3b7c4a, 9ac6bfbe), wired through identity.controller.ts (@UserId() actorUserId), and behaviour-tested (role-rank.spec + remove-role.handler.spec, 57 identity-security tests green). No fix was needed; the bugs were marked closed and Security health 85→95.
learnings.lesson: When the analyst imports a bug from lessons/docs into bugs.json, it MUST re-verify the bug still reproduces against the CURRENT source (read the file + run the spec) before recording status:open. A bug documented on date X may be fixed by commit Y before the QA pass. Trusting a stale lesson nearly caused (a) a false NO-GO-flavored health deduction and (b) a redundant 'fix' of already-correct security code. This is the source-of-truth corollary to LR-001: verify against code, not against a prior narrative.

learnings.note: Orchestrator initially mis-detected the run as a 'full audit' because a glob for '.qa-memory/**' returned no files — the default glob did not traverse the dot-prefixed directory. The scout (which lists directories directly) correctly found the existing .qa-memory. An out-of-band `ls -la .qa-memory` confirmed it existed (full memory base built earlier the same day).
learnings.lesson: Detect incremental-vs-full mode with a direct directory listing (ls -la .qa-memory) or a dotglob-enabled check, never a plain '**' glob — hidden dot-directories are skipped and you will falsely trigger a wasteful full rebuild. Verify mode before re-dispatching the analyst (LR-001).

learnings.note: Scout-reported 'untested ops' lists can have a high false-gap rate when the project has many controller-level spec files colocated with the controllers (apps/backend/src/api/public/*.spec.ts). Of the 26 originally-listed untested ops, 24 were false gaps — covered by controller specs, handler specs, or real-e2e tests. Always verify by reading the actual test files before reporting gaps.
learnings.lesson: Before reporting an API endpoint as untested, check: (1) colocated controller spec (*.controller.spec.ts), (2) handler spec (*.handler.spec.ts), (3) real-e2e spec, (4) website/dashboard unit test that calls the API client method. The scout's parser may miss colocated specs.

learnings.note: Two real API gaps confirmed after full verification: POST /public/programs/{id}/enroll (no controller-level or e2e test — only handler unit test) and GET /public/intake-forms/applicable (intake-forms.controller.ts has no spec file at all). Both are P2.
learnings.lesson: The enroll endpoint gap is notable: the handler has a spec but the HTTP wiring (controller → handler → response shape) is untested. A controller spec that mocks the handler and asserts the HTTP contract is the missing piece.

learnings.note: The RECEPTIONIST payment/invoice access is correctly 200 (built-in-rules.ts:30 grants RECEPTIONIST create+read on Payment AND Invoice). The flows RBAC spec (rbac-route-access.spec.ts) is STALE on this point. When an RBAC assertion fails on a status mismatch, always diff against built-in-rules.ts before classifying as a security gap.
learnings.lesson: built-in-rules.ts is the authoritative CASL contract. Stale e2e RBAC assertions are test-maintenance items, not security bugs. The casl-ability.factory.spec and route-auth-coverage.e2e-spec are the authoritative test sources.

learnings.note: Two P1 security gaps documented in lessons.md (2026-06-23): (1) assertCanAssignRole uses <= instead of < — SUPER_ADMIN cannot grant SUPER_ADMIN role. (2) RemoveRoleHandler lacks rank gate — low-rank actor can strip higher-ranked user's custom role. Both require owner approval before fix. Not blocking for non-role-management releases.
learnings.lesson: Security gaps in role-rank logic must be surfaced in coverage.json and releases.json even when they cannot be fixed without owner approval. The health score deduction (85 instead of 100 for security) reflects this.

learnings.note: Dashboard e2e flows (27 specs) are documented-flaky due to: (1) dev-server crashes under load (Turbopack instability), (2) test-data pollution from ensure* helpers without afterAll cleanup, (3) stale selectors (guessed Arabic text, class-based selectors). These are test-infrastructure debt, not product bugs. The reliable signal is: backend real-e2e (17 specs/141 tests) + dashboard Vitest (171/1499) + website Vitest (63/474) + smoke (7/39).
learnings.lesson: For a trustworthy dashboard e2e signal: (1) run against next start (prod build), not next dev; (2) ensure DB is clean before run; (3) fix selectors to use role/test-id attributes. Until then, treat flows as 'have tests by enumeration' but not as a reliable pass/fail signal.

learnings.note: The API coverage 'any-test' number (99.3%) is much higher than the 'behaviour-only' number (72.0%) because the route-auth-coverage.e2e-spec covers 224 routes with anonymous 401 probes. This is a valid and important security test but not a behaviour test. Always report both numbers and label them clearly.
learnings.lesson: Report API coverage with two numbers: (1) any-test (includes gate probes) and (2) behaviour-only (excludes anonymous 401 probes). The behaviour-only number is more meaningful for functional coverage; the gate-probe number is more meaningful for security coverage.
